cases.description: GitPython ≤3.1.29 — insufficient sanitization of user-supplied arguments passed to git commands via shell=True.

findings.title: CWE-78: OS Command Injection in clone_repo()
findings.description: Untrusted data flows from `repo_url` (HTTP query parameter, L9) to `subprocess.run()` at L11 without sanitization. An attacker can exploit this to execute arbitrary OS commands on the server.
findings.suggestion: Pass a list to subprocess (no shell=True): `subprocess.run(['cmd', arg])`

rule.title: Python command injection via tainted shell execution
rule.summary: Tracks user-controlled values flowing into shell or command execution sinks.
rule.remediation: Use argument arrays, avoid shell=True, and allowlist executable inputs.

findings.title: CWE-78: Command injection in clone_repo() via shell=True
findings.description: `clone_repo()` calls `subprocess.run()` with `shell=True` and a dynamically constructed command at L11. An attacker can inject arbitrary OS commands via shell metacharacters (`;`, `$(...)`, `|`).
findings.suggestion: Pass a list to subprocess instead: `subprocess.run(['cmd', arg], ...)`. If shell=True is unavoidable, use `shlex.quote()` on every user-controlled part.

rule.title: Python shell command built from dynamic input
rule.summary: Flags `subprocess` usage that combines `shell=True` with attacker-influenced command text.
rule.remediation: Use `shell=False`, argument arrays, and strict allowlists for external commands.

cases.description: oauthlib ≤3.2.1 — SSRF / redirect to attacker-controlled server via unvalidated redirect_uri in OAuth flow.

findings.title: CWE-918: Server-Side Request Forgery in oauth_callback()
findings.description: Untrusted data flows from `callback_url` (HTTP query parameter, L9) to `requests.post()` at L11 without sanitization. An attacker can exploit this to make the server connect to internal services or arbitrary URLs.
findings.suggestion: Validate URLs against an allowlist of permitted schemes and hosts.
findings.auto_fix:
BEFORE: token_response = requests.post(callback_url, data={"grant_type": "authorization_code"})
AFTER:      from urllib.parse import urlparse
            parsed = urlparse(url)
            if parsed.hostname not in ALLOWED_HOSTS:
                raise ValueError("URL not in allowlist")
            token_response = requests.post(callback_url, data={"grant_type": "authorization_code"})

rule.title: Python SSRF via tainted outbound URL
rule.summary: Tracks user-controlled URLs into outbound HTTP clients.
rule.remediation: Validate hosts against an allowlist and block internal/private destinations.

findings.title: CWE-918: SSRF in oauth_callback() — unvalidated URL passed to post()
findings.description: `oauth_callback()` calls `post()` with `callback_url` at L11. If this comes from user input, an attacker can reach internal services (cloud metadata 169.254.169.254, Redis, databases) or arbitrary external URLs.
findings.suggestion: Validate URL against an allowlist: parse with `urllib.parse.urlparse()`, verify scheme in ('http','https') and netloc in ALLOWED_HOSTS. Block private/loopback IP ranges.
findings.auto_fix:
BEFORE: token_response = requests.post(callback_url, data={"grant_type": "authorization_code"})
AFTER:      from urllib.parse import urlparse
            parsed = urlparse(url)
            if parsed.hostname not in ALLOWED_HOSTS:
                raise ValueError("URL not in allowlist")
            token_response = requests.post(callback_url, data={"grant_type": "authorization_code"})

rule.title: Python outbound HTTP call with unvalidated variable URL
rule.summary: Flags HTTP client calls whose destination URL is carried in a variable without allowlist validation.
rule.remediation: Validate destinations against a hostname allowlist and reject internal/private targets.

findings.title: CWE-307: No rate limiting on authentication route at line 7
findings.description: Authentication route at L7 has no rate-limiting decorator or middleware in scope: `@app.route("/oauth/callback")`. An attacker can brute-force credentials, OTPs, or tokens.
findings.suggestion: Add rate limiting: `flask-limiter` for Flask (`@limiter.limit('5/minute')`), `slowapi` for FastAPI, or a middleware-level rate limiter.

rule.title: Python auth/sensitive route missing rate limiting
rule.summary: Flags Flask/FastAPI/Django authentication or sensitive routes (login, MFA, password-reset, token-refresh) where no rate-limiting library is detected in file scope, enabling brute-force attacks.
rule.remediation: Use `flask-limiter` (`@limiter.limit('5/minute')`), `slowapi` for FastAPI, `django-ratelimit`, or a reverse-proxy rate limiter in front of sensitive endpoints.

cases.description: Django 1.11–2.2 — SQL injection via JSONField keys used in queryset filters when passed as untrusted keyword arguments.

findings.title: CWE-89: SQL Injection in search()
findings.description: Untrusted data flows from `name` (HTTP query parameter, L10) to `execute()` at L12 without sanitization. An attacker can exploit this to execute arbitrary SQL, read/modify/delete database records.
findings.suggestion: Use parameterized queries: `cursor.execute('SELECT ... WHERE id = ?', (uid,))`
findings.auto_fix:
BEFORE: rows = db.execute(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '{name}'").fetchall()
AFTER:      cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '?", (name,))

rule.title: Python SQL injection via tainted query text
rule.summary: Tracks user input into SQL text formatting and execution calls.
rule.remediation: Switch to parameterized SQL or ORM bind parameters.

findings.title: CWE-862: Missing authentication on search() — state-mutating route with no authentication
findings.description: `search()` is a route handler with no authentication decorator. Any unauthenticated caller can reach this endpoint. Missing: `@login_required` or equivalent.
findings.suggestion: Add `@login_required` above `@app.route`. For admin routes, also verify elevated role.

trace.label: state mutation in route body reachable without auth

rule.title: Python route missing authentication
rule.summary: Flags Flask/FastAPI-style routes with risky behavior and no auth guard.
rule.remediation: Add `@login_required` or equivalent auth middleware before route execution.
known_limitations: Heuristics are strongest on common Flask/FastAPI and Express/Router-style patterns.

findings.title: CWE-22: Path Traversal via os.path.join() in download()
findings.description: Untrusted data flows from `filename` (HTTP query parameter, L10) to `os.path.join()` at L12 without sanitization. An attacker can exploit this to read/write arbitrary files on the server.
findings.suggestion: Sanitize paths: `secure_filename(f)` then `assert resolved.startswith(BASE_DIR)`

rule.title: Python SQL injection via tainted query text
rule.summary: Tracks user input into SQL text formatting and execution calls.
rule.remediation: Switch to parameterized SQL or ORM bind parameters.

findings.title: CWE-22: Path traversal in download() via os.path.join()
findings.description: `download()` passes `filename` (dynamic/path-like input) to `os.path.join()` at L12 without sanitization. `../` sequences can escape the intended directory.
findings.suggestion: Sanitize: `from werkzeug.utils import secure_filename; safe = secure_filename(filename)`. Verify resolved path: `assert os.path.realpath(p).startswith(BASE_DIR)`.

rule.title: Python path join with unsanitized user input
rule.summary: Flags `os.path.join` or similar path composition with tainted user input.
rule.remediation: Normalize against a safe root and reject any resolved path that escapes it.

cases.description: Celery — deserialization of untrusted data via pickle backend allows RCE from a compromised broker.

findings.title: CWE-502: Unsafe deserialization at line 12
findings.description: Unsafe pickle deserialization at L12: `task = pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(task_data))`. If the data comes from an untrusted source, an attacker can achieve RCE.
findings.suggestion: Use JSON. If pickle is required, verify data integrity with HMAC before loading.
findings.auto_fix:
BEFORE: task = pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(task_data))
AFTER:      task = json.loads(base64.b64decode(task_data))

rule.title: Python unsafe deserialization pattern
rule.summary: Flags direct use of unsafe deserializers such as `pickle.loads`, `marshal.loads`, or unsafe `yaml.load`.
rule.remediation: Use JSON or verify integrity and safety before deserializing.

findings.title: Insecure deserialization via pickle with tainted data
findings.description: User-controlled data is passed to 'pickle.loads'. pickle deserialisation executes arbitrary Python code, enabling remote code execution if an attacker controls the serialised payload.
findings.suggestion: Never deserialise untrusted data with pickle. Use json.loads() for data interchange, or cryptographically sign and verify the payload before deserialisation.
findings.auto_fix:
BEFORE: task = pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(task_data))
AFTER:      task = json.loads(base64.b64decode(task_data))

rule.title: Insecure deserialization via pickle with tainted data
rule.summary: Flags use of deserializers capable of instantiating attacker-controlled objects.
rule.remediation: Prefer pure-data formats like JSON, or verify integrity before deserializing unsafe formats.

findings.title: CWE-502: Unsafe Deserialization in run_task()
findings.description: Untrusted data flows from `task_data` (parsed HTTP JSON body (Flask get_json()), L11) to `pickle.loads()` at L12 without sanitization. An attacker can exploit this to execute arbitrary code via crafted serialized objects.
findings.suggestion: Use json.loads() for untrusted data. If pickle is required, verify HMAC first.
findings.auto_fix:
BEFORE: task = pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(task_data))
AFTER:      task = json.loads(base64.b64decode(task_data))

rule.title: Python unsafe deserialization via tainted input
rule.summary: Tracks untrusted data into unsafe deserializers such as pickle or yaml.load-style sinks.
rule.remediation: Prefer JSON or verify integrity before deserializing attacker-controlled content.

findings.suggestion: Add `@login_required` above `@app.route`. For admin routes, also verify elevated role.

cases.description: Application uses MD5 to hash user passwords, making them trivially crackable via rainbow tables or GPU brute-force.

findings.title: CWE-327: Weak password hashing (MD5) at line 13
findings.description: `MD5` is cryptographically broken for password storage at L13. No salt is used; rainbow tables and GPU brute-force make this trivial to crack.
findings.suggestion: Use bcrypt, argon2, or scrypt: `bcrypt.hashpw(password.encode(), bcrypt.gensalt())`
findings.auto_fix:
BEFORE: hashed = hashlib.md5(password.encode()).hexdigest()
AFTER:      import bcrypt
            hashed = bcrypt.hashpw(password.encode(, bcrypt.gensalt())

rule.title: Python weak password hashing
rule.summary: Flags MD5/SHA1/SHA224 used for password hashing or similar credential protection.
rule.remediation: Use bcrypt, argon2, or scrypt for passwords instead of fast general-purpose hashes.

findings.description: Registration route at L9 has no rate-limiting decorator or middleware in scope: `@app.route("/register", methods=["POST"])`. An attacker can brute-force credentials, OTPs, or tokens.
findings.suggestion: Add rate limiting: `flask-limiter` for Flask (`@limiter.limit('5/minute')`), `slowapi` for FastAPI, or a middleware-level rate limiter.

rule.remediation: Use `flask-limiter` (`@limiter.limit('5/minute')`), `slowapi` for FastAPI, `django-ratelimit`, or a reverse-proxy rate limiter in front of sensitive endpoints.

cases.description: Hardcoded AWS credentials in source code.

findings.title: CWE-798: Hardcoded AWS credential at line 4
findings.description: A AWS credential is hardcoded in source code at L4. This is visible in version control. Rotate this credential immediately.
findings.suggestion: Use environment variables or a secrets manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, .env excluded from git).
findings.auto_fix:
BEFORE: AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID = "AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE"
AFTER:  AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID = os.environ["AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID"]

rule.title: Python hardcoded credential
rule.summary: Flags likely credentials, API keys, or signing secrets committed directly to source.
rule.remediation: Move secrets to environment variables or a secrets manager and rotate any exposed values.

cases.description: IDOR — authenticated route returns any user's document by ID without ownership check.

findings.title: CWE-287: Auth bypass in login_required() — token presence check only, no validation
findings.description: `login_required()` assigns `token` from `request.headers/cookies.get()` and gates access on `if token:` — checking only that the header EXISTS. Any non-empty string bypasses the check. Token is never validated.
findings.suggestion: Validate the token: decode a JWT with signature verification, compare an HMAC, or look up an opaque token in a database. Never gate on presence alone.

rule.title: Python decorator auth bypass via token presence only
rule.summary: Flags `@wraps`-style auth decorators that gate access on token/header presence without verification.
rule.remediation: Decode and verify credentials inside the decorator before calling the protected handler.

findings.title: CWE-639: IDOR in get_doc() — resource fetched by ID with no ownership check
findings.description: `get_doc()` queries a resource by `id` at L23 without verifying the requesting user owns it. Any authenticated user can retrieve another user's data by substituting any `id`. SQL: `SELECT * FROM docs WHERE id = ?`.
findings.suggestion: Add an ownership filter: `WHERE id = ? AND owner_id = ?`, pass `(doc_id, g.user_id)` as parameters.

trace.label: resource query `SELECT * FROM docs WHERE id = ?`

rule.title: Python route-level IDOR
rule.summary: Flags authenticated resource lookups by ID with no owner or tenant restriction.
rule.remediation: Add owner filters to the lookup or perform a verified ownership guard before returning data.
known_limitations: Heuristic route analysis does not prove exploitability when ownership is enforced in external services.

findings_considered_payload.title: CWE-639: IDOR in get_doc() — resource fetched by ID with no ownership check
findings_considered_payload.description: `get_doc()` queries a resource by `id` at L23 without verifying the requesting user owns it. Any authenticated user can retrieve another user's data by substituting any `id`. SQL: `SELECT * FROM docs WHERE id = ?`.
findings_considered_payload.suggestion: Add an ownership filter: `WHERE id = ? AND owner_id = ?`, pass `(doc_id, g.user_id)` as parameters.

trace.label: resource query `SELECT * FROM docs WHERE id = ?`

rule.title: Python route-level IDOR
rule.summary: Flags authenticated resource lookups by ID with no owner or tenant restriction.
rule.remediation: Add owner filters to the lookup or perform a verified ownership guard before returning data.
known_limitations: Heuristic route analysis does not prove exploitability when ownership is enforced in external services.

cases.description: Auth bypass — decorator checks token presence only, never validates the value.

findings.title: CWE-287: Auth bypass in require_auth() — token presence check only, no validation
findings.description: `require_auth()` assigns `token` from `request.headers/cookies.get()` and gates access on `if token:` — checking only that the header EXISTS. Any non-empty string bypasses the check. Token is never validated.
findings.suggestion: Validate the token: decode a JWT with signature verification, compare an HMAC, or look up an opaque token in a database. Never gate on presence alone.

rule.title: Python decorator auth bypass via token presence only
rule.summary: Flags `@wraps`-style auth decorators that gate access on token/header presence without verification.
rule.remediation: Decode and verify credentials inside the decorator before calling the protected handler.

findings.title: CWE-285: Broken access control in admin_users() — admin endpoint with no privilege check
findings.description: `admin_users()` is auth-protected but never verifies that the caller holds an admin role or permission. Any authenticated user can reach this privileged route.
findings.suggestion: Add a privilege decorator such as `@admin_required` / `@requires_role('admin')`, or an explicit guard like `if not current_user.is_admin: abort(403)`. Never rely on authentication alone for admin routes.

trace.label: no privilege decorator or inline role/permission guard detected
admin route reachable after auth only

rule.title: Python admin route missing privilege guard
rule.summary: Flags admin-like routes that authenticate callers but never verify elevated role or permission.
rule.remediation: Add a role decorator or explicit admin/permission check before privileged logic.

cases.description: Silent exception swallowing hides critical errors.

findings.title: Silent exception swallowing in delete_user_file()
findings.description: `delete_user_file()` catches Exception with `pass` at L7, hiding disk I/O failures, permission errors, data corruption, and any other exception.
findings.suggestion: Log and re-raise: `logger.exception('Unexpected error'); raise`
findings.auto_fix:
BEFORE: except Exception:
AFTER:      logger.exception("Unexpected error")
            raise

rule.title: Silent exception swallowing
rule.summary: Flags broad exception handlers that swallow failures with `pass` or `continue`.
rule.remediation: Log and re-raise unexpected errors or catch only specific exceptions you can safely handle.

cases.description: yaml.load() without SafeLoader allows arbitrary Python object construction.

findings.title: CWE-502: Unsafe deserialization at line 11
findings.description: Unsafe yaml.load without SafeLoader at L11: `config = yaml.load(data)`. If the data comes from an untrusted source, an attacker can achieve RCE.
findings.suggestion: Use JSON. If pickle is required, verify data integrity with HMAC before loading.

rule.title: Python unsafe deserialization pattern
rule.summary: Flags direct use of unsafe deserializers such as `pickle.loads`, `marshal.loads`, or unsafe `yaml.load`.
rule.remediation: Use JSON or verify integrity and safety before deserializing.

findings.title: CWE-862: Missing authentication on load_config() — state-mutating route with no authentication
findings.description: `load_config()` is a route handler with no authentication decorator. Any unauthenticated caller can reach this endpoint. Missing: `@login_required` or equivalent.
findings.suggestion: Add `@login_required` above `@app.route`. For admin routes, also verify elevated role.

rule.title: Python route missing authentication
rule.summary: Flags Flask/FastAPI-style routes with risky behavior and no auth guard.
rule.remediation: Add `@login_required` or equivalent auth middleware before route execution.
known_limitations: Heuristics are strongest on common Flask/FastAPI and Express/Router-style patterns.

cases.description: SQL injection via template literal in database query.

findings.title: CWE-89: SQL injection via string concatenation at line 10
findings.description: SQL query assembled with string concatenation at L10: `const [rows] = await db.query(`SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = '${userId}'`);`. An attacker can break out of the query context and read, modify, or delete data.
findings.suggestion: Use parameterized queries: `db.query('SELECT ... WHERE id = $1', [id])`.

rule.title: SQL injection via string concatenation
rule.summary: SQL query assembled with string concatenation.
rule.remediation: Use parameterized queries: `db.query('SELECT ... WHERE id = $1', [id])`.

findings.title: CWE-89: SQL injection via template literal query at line 10
findings.description: SQL execution at L10 uses a template literal with interpolation: `db.query(`SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = '${userId}'`)`. This is equivalent to building SQL with string concatenation.
findings.suggestion: Use parameterized queries with placeholders instead of interpolating data into SQL text.

rule.title: SQL injection via template literal query
rule.summary: SQL query uses a template literal with interpolation.
rule.remediation: Replace template literal with a parameterized placeholder: `WHERE id = $1`.

findings.title: CWE-798: JWT signed with hardcoded secret at line 9
findings.description: `jwt.sign()` uses a hardcoded string as the secret at L9: `const token = jwt.sign({ username }, 'mysecretpassword123', { expiresIn: '1h' })`. Anyone with the source can forge tokens.

findings.suggestion: Scope lookups by owner/tenant as well as resource ID, for example `where: { id: postId, ownerId: request.user.id }`, and protect the route with auth middleware.

rule.title: JS route-level IDOR or public IDOR
rule.summary: Flags route handlers that load resources by route ID without ownership checks, with or without auth.
rule.remediation: Scope resource lookups by owner or tenant and protect public routes with authentication.
known_limitations: Heuristic route analysis is strongest on common Express/Fastify/Koa/Nest patterns.

findings.title: CWE-285: Missing ownership check before mutation at line 12
findings.description: Authenticated route `/posts/:postId` mutates a resource at L12 using a user-controlled route identifier without verifying the caller owns that resource.
findings.suggestion: Load the record with an owner/tenant filter before mutating it, for example `where: { id: postId, ownerId: request.user.id }`, or block with a 403 ownership check.

trace.label: no ownership guard detected before mutation

rule.title: JS mutation missing ownership check
rule.summary: Flags route mutations on resource IDs where no ownership check precedes the change.
rule.remediation: Load the resource, verify ownership or role, and mutate only after the guard passes.

cases.description: Express route passes user-supplied filename to res.sendFile without path validation.

findings.title: CWE-22: Path traversal in dynamic static-file serving at line 8
findings.description: `sendFile`, `createReadStream`, or `res.download` called with a user-controlled path at L8: `res.sendFile(filename, { root: '/var/uploads' });`. An attacker can traverse the directory with `../` sequences.
findings.suggestion: Resolve the path inside a safe base directory and reject requests that escape it: `path.resolve(BASE, file).startsWith(BASE)`.

rule.title: Path traversal in dynamic static-file serving
rule.summary: `sendFile`, `createReadStream`, or `res.download` called with a user-controlled path.
rule.remediation: Resolve the path inside a safe base directory and reject requests that escape it: `path.resolve(BASE, file).startsWith(BASE)`.

cases.description: Flask login route has no rate-limiting, enabling brute-force attacks.

findings.title: CWE-307: No rate limiting on authentication route at line 6
findings.description: Authentication route at L6 has no rate-limiting decorator or middleware in scope: `@app.route('/login', methods=['POST'])`. An attacker can brute-force credentials, OTPs, or tokens.
findings.suggestion: Add rate limiting: `flask-limiter` for Flask (`@limiter.limit('5/minute')`), `slowapi` for FastAPI, or a middleware-level rate limiter.

rule.title: Python auth/sensitive route missing rate limiting
rule.summary: Flags Flask/FastAPI/Django authentication or sensitive routes (login, MFA, password-reset, token-refresh) where no rate-limiting library is detected in file scope, enabling brute-force attacks.
rule.remediation: Use `flask-limiter` (`@limiter.limit('5/minute')`), `slowapi` for FastAPI, `django-ratelimit`, or a reverse-proxy rate limiter in front of sensitive endpoints.

findings.title: CWE-79: Cross-Site Scripting (template injection) in render_page()
findings.description: Untrusted data flows from `template` (HTTP query parameter, L8) to `render_template_string()` at L9 without sanitization. An attacker can exploit this to inject malicious scripts that run in other users' browsers.
findings.suggestion: Use auto-escaping templates; never pass user input to render_template_string.

rule.title: Python XSS via tainted HTML response sink
rule.summary: Tracks untrusted data into HTML-rendering response sinks without sanitization.
rule.remediation: Escape output by default and sanitize trusted HTML fragments before rendering.
known_limitations: DOM and template heuristics are strongest when flows remain inside a file or helper chain.

cases.description: subprocess.getoutput() implicitly uses shell=True, enabling command injection via user-supplied filenames.

findings.title: CWE-78: OS Command Injection (shell=True implicit) in checksum()
findings.description: Untrusted data flows from `filename` (HTTP query parameter, L9) to `subprocess.getoutput()` at L10 without sanitization. An attacker can exploit this to execute arbitrary OS commands on the server.
findings.suggestion: Pass a list to subprocess (no shell=True): `subprocess.run(['cmd', arg])`

rule.title: Python command injection via tainted shell execution
rule.summary: Tracks user-controlled values flowing into shell or command execution sinks.
rule.remediation: Use argument arrays, avoid shell=True, and allowlist executable inputs.

findings.title: CWE-918: Server-Side Request Forgery in oauth_callback()
findings.description: Untrusted data flows from `callback_url` (HTTP query parameter, L9) to `requests.post()` at L11 without sanitization. An attacker can exploit this to make the server connect to internal services or arbitrary URLs.
findings.suggestion: Validate URLs against an allowlist of permitted schemes and hosts.
findings.auto_fix:
BEFORE: token_response = requests.post(callback_url, data={"grant_type": "authorization_code"})
AFTER:      from urllib.parse import urlparse
            parsed = urlparse(url)
            if parsed.hostname not in ALLOWED_HOSTS:
                raise ValueError("URL not in allowlist")
            token_response = requests.post(callback_url, data={"grant_type": "authorization_code"})

findings.title: CWE-918: SSRF in oauth_callback() — unvalidated URL passed to post()

rule.title: Python outbound HTTP call with unvalidated variable URL

rule.remediation: Validate destinations against a hostname allowlist and reject internal/private targets.

findings.title: CWE-307: No rate limiting on authentication route at line 7

cases.description: Django 1.11–2.2 — SQL injection via JSONField keys used in queryset filters when passed as untrusted keyword arguments.

findings.title: CWE-89: SQL Injection in search()
findings.description: Untrusted data flows from `name` (HTTP query parameter, L10) to `execute()` at L12 without sanitization. An attacker can exploit this to execute arbitrary SQL, read/modify/delete database records.
findings.suggestion: Use parameterized queries: `cursor.execute('SELECT ... WHERE id = ?', (uid,))`
findings.auto_fix:
BEFORE: rows = db.execute(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '{name}'").fetchall()
AFTER:      cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '?", (name,))

rule.title: Python SQL injection via tainted query text

cases.description: Celery — deserialization of untrusted data via pickle backend allows RCE from a compromised broker.

findings.title: CWE-502: Unsafe deserialization at line 12
findings.description: Unsafe pickle deserialization at L12: `task = pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(task_data))`. If the data comes from an untrusted source, an attacker can achieve RCE.
findings.suggestion: Use JSON. If pickle is required, verify data integrity with HMAC before loading.
findings.auto_fix:
BEFORE: task = pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(task_data))
AFTER:      task = json.loads(base64.b64decode(task_data))

rule.title: Python unsafe deserialization pattern
rule.summary: Flags direct use of unsafe deserializers such as `pickle.loads`, `marshal.loads`, or unsafe `yaml.load`.
rule.remediation: Use JSON or verify integrity and safety before deserializing.

findings.title: Insecure deserialization via pickle with tainted data
findings.description: User-controlled data is passed to 'pickle.loads'. pickle deserialisation executes arbitrary Python code, enabling remote code execution if an attacker controls the serialised payload.
findings.suggestion: Never deserialise untrusted data with pickle. Use json.loads() for data interchange, or cryptographically sign and verify the payload before deserialisation.
findings.auto_fix:
BEFORE: task = pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(task_data))
AFTER:      task = json.loads(base64.b64decode(task_data))

rule.title: Insecure deserialization via pickle with tainted data
rule.summary: Flags use of deserializers capable of instantiating attacker-controlled objects.
rule.remediation: Prefer pure-data formats like JSON, or verify integrity before deserializing unsafe formats.

findings.title: CWE-327: Weak password hashing (MD5) at line 13
findings.description: `MD5` is cryptographically broken for password storage at L13. No salt is used; rainbow tables and GPU brute-force make this trivial to crack.

BEFORE: hashed = hashlib.md5(password.encode()).hexdigest()
AFTER:      import bcrypt
            hashed = bcrypt.hashpw(password.encode(, bcrypt.gensalt())

cases.description: IDOR — authenticated route returns any user's document by ID without ownership check.

findings.title: CWE-287: Auth bypass in login_required() — token presence check only, no validation
findings.description: `login_required()` assigns `token` from `request.headers/cookies.get()` and gates access on `if token:` — checking only that the header EXISTS. Any non-empty string bypasses the check. Token is never validated.
findings.suggestion: Validate the token: decode a JWT with signature verification, compare an HMAC, or look up an opaque token in a database. Never gate on presence alone.

rule.title: Python decorator auth bypass via token presence only
rule.summary: Flags `@wraps`-style auth decorators that gate access on token/header presence without verification.
rule.remediation: Decode and verify credentials inside the decorator before calling the protected handler.

findings.title: CWE-639: IDOR in get_doc() — resource fetched by ID with no ownership check
findings.description: `get_doc()` queries a resource by `id` at L23 without verifying the requesting user owns it. Any authenticated user can retrieve another user's data by substituting any `id`. SQL: `SELECT * FROM docs WHERE id = ?`.
findings.suggestion: Add an ownership filter: `WHERE id = ? AND owner_id = ?`, pass `(doc_id, g.user_id)` as parameters.

trace.label: resource query `SELECT * FROM docs WHERE id = ?`

rule.title: Python route-level IDOR
rule.summary: Flags authenticated resource lookups by ID with no owner or tenant restriction.
rule.remediation: Add owner filters to the lookup or perform a verified ownership guard before returning data.
known_limitations: Heuristic route analysis does not prove exploitability when ownership is enforced in external services.

cases.description: Auth bypass — decorator checks token presence only, never validates the value.

findings.title: CWE-287: Auth bypass in require_auth() — token presence check only, no validation
findings.description: `require_auth()` assigns `token` from `request.headers/cookies.get()` and gates access on `if token:` — checking only that the header EXISTS. Any non-empty string bypasses the check. Token is never validated.
findings.suggestion: Validate the token: decode a JWT with signature verification, compare an HMAC, or look up an opaque token in a database. Never gate on presence alone.

rule.title: Python decorator auth bypass via token presence only
rule.summary: Flags `@wraps`-style auth decorators that gate access on token/header presence without verification.
rule.remediation: Decode and verify credentials inside the decorator before calling the protected handler.

findings.title: CWE-285: Broken access control in admin_users() — admin endpoint with no privilege check
findings.description: `admin_users()` is auth-protected but never verifies that the caller holds an admin role or permission. Any authenticated user can reach this privileged route.
findings.suggestion: Add a privilege decorator such as `@admin_required` / `@requires_role('admin')`, or an explicit guard like `if not current_user.is_admin: abort(403)`. Never rely on authentication alone for admin routes.

trace.label: no privilege decorator or inline role/permission guard detected
admin route reachable after auth only

rule.title: Python admin route missing privilege guard
rule.summary: Flags admin-like routes that authenticate callers but never verify elevated role or permission.
rule.remediation: Add a role decorator or explicit admin/permission check before privileged logic.

cases.description: Silent exception swallowing hides critical errors.

findings.title: Silent exception swallowing in delete_user_file()
findings.description: `delete_user_file()` catches Exception with `pass` at L7, hiding disk I/O failures, permission errors, data corruption, and any other exception.
findings.suggestion: Log and re-raise: `logger.exception('Unexpected error'); raise`
findings.auto_fix:
BEFORE: except Exception:
AFTER:      logger.exception("Unexpected error")
            raise

rule.title: Silent exception swallowing
rule.summary: Flags broad exception handlers that swallow failures with `pass` or `continue`.
rule.remediation: Log and re-raise unexpected errors or catch only specific exceptions you can safely handle.

cases.description: yaml.load() without SafeLoader allows arbitrary Python object construction.

findings.title: CWE-502: Unsafe deserialization at line 11
findings.description: Unsafe yaml.load without SafeLoader at L11: `config = yaml.load(data)`. If the data comes from an untrusted source, an attacker can achieve RCE.
findings.suggestion: Use JSON. If pickle is required, verify data integrity with HMAC before loading.

rule.title: Python unsafe deserialization pattern
rule.summary: Flags direct use of unsafe deserializers such as `pickle.loads`, `marshal.loads`, or unsafe `yaml.load`.
rule.remediation: Use JSON or verify integrity and safety before deserializing.

findings.title: CWE-862: Missing authentication on load_config() — state-mutating route with no authentication
findings.description: `load_config()` is a route handler with no authentication decorator. Any unauthenticated caller can reach this endpoint. Missing: `@login_required` or equivalent.
findings.suggestion: Add `@login_required` above `@app.route`. For admin routes, also verify elevated role.

trace.label: mutating route methods `POST` reachable without auth

rule.title: Python route missing authentication
rule.summary: Flags Flask/FastAPI-style routes with risky behavior and no auth guard.
rule.remediation: Add `@login_required` or equivalent auth middleware before route execution.
known_limitations: Heuristics are strongest on common Flask/FastAPI and Express/Router-style patterns.

findings.title: Insecure YAML deserialization
findings.description: yaml.load() without Loader=yaml.SafeLoader can execute arbitrary Python code when processing untrusted YAML input. Loader used: 'default (unsafe)'.

findings.title: CWE-1321: Prototype pollution risk at line 9
findings.description: Object modification using `__proto__`, `constructor.prototype`, or `Object.assign` with unchecked user input at L9: `Object.assign(config, req.body);`.
findings.suggestion: Validate keys, strip `__proto__` / `constructor`, and prefer safe merge patterns.

rule.title: Prototype pollution risk
rule.summary: Object modification using `__proto__`, `constructor.prototype`, or `Object.assign` with unchecked user input.
rule.remediation: Validate keys, strip `__proto__` / `constructor`, and prefer safe merge patterns.

findings.title: CWE-1321: Prototype pollution via Object.assign/spread at line 9
findings.description: Spreading `req.body` directly into an object at L9: `Object.assign(config, req.body);`. A malicious `__proto__` key in the body contaminates all objects.
findings.suggestion: Sanitize keys first with a schema validator or strip `__proto__` / `constructor` keys before merging.

rule.title: JS dangerous object merge from request body
rule.summary: Flags raw `req.body` merges or spreads that can introduce prototype-polluting keys.
rule.remediation: Validate body shape and strip `__proto__` / `constructor` keys before merging.

cases.description: Stored XSS via innerHTML assignment — user-controlled content inserted into the DOM without sanitization.

findings.title: CWE-79: XSS via innerHTML assignment at line 6
findings.description: Unsanitized data assigned to `innerHTML` or `outerHTML` at L6: `document.getElementById('bio').innerHTML = user.bio;`. An attacker can inject a `<script>` tag or event handler that executes in the victim's browser.
findings.suggestion: Use `textContent` instead of `innerHTML`, or sanitize with DOMPurify.sanitize(). Never concatenate user data into HTML.

cases.description: SQL injection via template literal in database query.

findings.title: CWE-89: SQL injection via string concatenation at line 10
findings.description: SQL query assembled with string concatenation at L10: `const [rows] = await db.query(`SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = '${userId}'`);`. An attacker can break out of the query context and read, modify, or delete data.
findings.suggestion: Use parameterized queries: `db.query('SELECT ... WHERE id = $1', [id])`.

rule.title: SQL injection via string concatenation
rule.summary: SQL query assembled with string concatenation.
rule.remediation: Use parameterized queries: `db.query('SELECT ... WHERE id = $1', [id])`.

findings.title: CWE-89: SQL injection via template literal query at line 10
findings.description: SQL execution at L10 uses a template literal with interpolation: `db.query(`SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = '${userId}'`)`. This is equivalent to building SQL with string concatenation.
findings.suggestion: Use parameterized queries with placeholders instead of interpolating data into SQL text.

rule.title: SQL injection via template literal query
rule.summary: SQL query uses a template literal with interpolation.
rule.remediation: Replace template literal with a parameterized placeholder: `WHERE id = $1`.

rule.remediation: Add verified auth middleware, guard decorators, or JWT/session validation before route logic.
known_limitations: Heuristics are strongest on common Flask/FastAPI and Express/Router-style patterns.

cases.description: JWT signed with a hardcoded weak secret.

findings.title: CWE-798: JWT signed with hardcoded secret at line 9
findings.description: `jwt.sign()` uses a hardcoded string as the secret at L9: `const token = jwt.sign({ username }, 'mysecretpassword123', { expiresIn: '1h' })`. Anyone with the source can forge tokens.
findings.suggestion: Move the secret to `process.env.JWT_SECRET` and load it at startup.

rule.title: JS hardcoded JWT signing secret
rule.summary: Flags `jwt.sign()` calls that use inline string secrets instead of externalized key material.
rule.remediation: Move the signing secret to environment or secret-manager storage and rotate exposed values.

findings.title: CWE-307: No rate limiting on authentication route at line 6
findings.description: Authentication route at L6 (`app.post('/login', (req, res) => {`) has no rate-limiting middleware in scope. An attacker can brute-force credentials, OTPs, or tokens.
findings.suggestion: Apply rate limiting, for example `const limiter = rateLimit({ windowMs: 15*60*1000, max: 10 })`, before the auth handler.

rule.title: JS auth route missing rate limiting
rule.summary: Flags login-like routes with no obvious rate-limiter middleware in scope.
rule.remediation: Apply a per-IP or per-account rate limiter before the auth handler.

cases.description: Express route returns an arbitrary profile by route ID without auth or ownership scoping.

findings.title: CWE-639: Public IDOR via route parameter at line 7
findings.description: Route `/profiles/:id` performs a resource lookup at L7 using a user-controlled route identifier without authentication or ownership scoping. An attacker can access another user's record by changing the ID.
findings.suggestion: Scope lookups by owner/tenant as well as resource ID, for example `where: { id: postId, ownerId: request.user.id }`, and protect the route with auth middleware.

rule.title: JS route-level IDOR or public IDOR
rule.summary: Flags route handlers that load resources by route ID without ownership checks, with or without auth.
rule.remediation: Scope resource lookups by owner or tenant and protect public routes with authentication.
known_limitations: Heuristic route analysis is strongest on common Express/Fastify/Koa/Nest patterns.

findings.title: CWE-22: Path traversal in dynamic static-file serving at line 8
findings.description: `sendFile`, `createReadStream`, or `res.download` called with a user-controlled path at L8: `res.sendFile(filename, { root: '/var/uploads' });`. An attacker can traverse the directory with `../` sequences.
findings.suggestion: Resolve the path inside a safe base directory and reject requests that escape it: `path.resolve(BASE, file).startsWith(BASE)`.

rule.remediation: Use `flask-limiter` (`@limiter.limit('5/minute')`), `slowapi` for FastAPI, `django-ratelimit`, or a reverse-proxy rate limiter in front of sensitive endpoints.

findings.title: CWE-22: Path traversal in clone() via os.path.join()
findings.description: `clone()` passes `VSI_MEM_FILESYSTEM_BASE_PATH` (dynamic/path-like input) to `os.path.join()` at L470 without sanitization. `../` sequences can escape the intended directory.
findings.suggestion: Sanitize: `from werkzeug.utils import secure_filename; safe = secure_filename(filename)`. Verify resolved path: `assert os.path.realpath(p).startswith(BASE_DIR)`.

rule.title: Python path join with unsanitized user input
rule.summary: Flags `os.path.join` or similar path composition with tainted user input.
rule.remediation: Normalize against a safe root and reject any resolved path that escapes it.

findings.suggestion: Sanitize paths: `secure_filename(f)` then `assert resolved.startswith(BASE_DIR)`
